request.question: In what year was Rabun Gap-Nacoochee School's 1966 magazine project collected and published in book form?

title: When the Twerms Came
Rabun Gap-Nacoochee School
Foxfire (magazine)
Andrew Jackson Ritchie
Roald Dahl: Collected Stories
Alessandro Ludovico
The Headmaster (short story)
Sanshirō (novel)
The Queen of Zamba
Gnome Press
sentences: "When the Twerms Came" is a short story by British author Arthur C. Clarke published in the May 1972 edition of "Playboy" magazine.

It deals with an invasion of Earth one wet Tuesday afternoon by deadly Twerms and was published in book form in "The View from Serendip" and "More Than One Universe" collections, but was not included in the "The Collected Stories of Arthur C. Clarke".
Rabun Gap-Nacoochee School (informally known as Rabun Gap) is a small, private college preparatory school located in Rabun County, Georgia, United States, in the Appalachian Mountains.
It is both a boarding and a day school.

Rabun Gap is notable for initiating the "Foxfire" magazine project in 1966, experiential education based on interviewing local people, and writing and publishing articles about their stories and oral traditions.
This inspired numerous schools across the country to develop similar programs.

The Foxfire magazine began in 1966, written and published as a quarterly American magazine by students at Rabun Gap-Nacoochee School, a private secondary education school located in the U.S. state of Georgia.
At the time Foxfire began, Rabun Gap Nacoochee School was also operating as a public secondary education school for students who were residents of northern Rabun County, Georgia.
An example of experiential education, the magazine had articles based on the students' interviews with local people about aspects and practices in Appalachian culture.
They captured oral history, craft traditions, and other material about the culture.
When the articles were collected and published in book form in 1972, it became a bestseller nationally and gained attention for the Foxfire project.
Andrew Jackson Ritchie (January 30, 1868–1948) was the founder of Rabun Gap Industrial School, which later merged with Nacoochee Institute to become Rabun Gap-Nacoochee School.
He served there as president until 1939.
Roald Dahl: Collected Stories is a hardcover edition of short-stories by Roald Dahl for adults.
It was published in the US in October 2006 by Random House as part of the Everyman Library.
The present volume includes for the first time all the stories in chronological order as established by Dahl's biographer, Jeremy Treglown, in consultation with the Dahl estate.
A few of the short stories were not published chronologically in book form, but appeared later, collected in "More Tales of the Unexpected" (1980).
The collection contains all of the short stories published in the following collections:
Alessandro Ludovico is a researcher, artist and chief editor of Neural magazine since 1993.
He received his Ph.D. degree in English and Media from Anglia Ruskin University in Cambridge (UK).
He is Associate Professor at the Winchester School of Art, University of Southampton and Lecturer at Parsons Paris - The New School.
He has published and edited several books, and has lectured worldwide.
He also served as an advisor for the Documenta 12's Magazine Project.
He is one of the authors of the award-winning Hacking Monopolism trilogy of artworks (Google Will Eat Itself), Amazon Noir), Face to Facebook).
The Headmaster is a short story by the British mystery and thriller writer Michael Gilbert about the counterspies Mr. Calder and Mr. Behrens.

First published in the United Kingdom in the June, 1962, issue of "Argosy", it was later published in the United States in "Ellery Queen's Mystery Magazine" and then collected in book form along with other stories about the same two protagonists as the sixth of eleven stories in "Game without Rules".

It is set in a contemporary, but undated London, and, unlike most of the other stories in this series, has Mr. Calder as the main character, with only brief appearances by Mr. Behrens and Rasselas, the Persian deerhound.

It is written in Gilbert's usual spare style, but with even fewer overtones and descriptions of the characters than are found in most of his works; the plot itself, and its resolution, is also somewhat unlikely, so the story, compared to the others in the series, must be considered a fairly minor one.
It does, however, have several examples of Gilbert's understated and astringent humor.
Sanshirō (三四郎 ) is a full-length novel by the Japanese writer Natsume Sōseki.
The novel was originally published as a serialized work in the Japanese newspaper Asahi Shimbun from September 1 through December 29 of 1908.
The Shunyōdō Shoten Company published it in book form in May 1909.
The book form is divided into 13 chapters.
Sanshirō is the first in a trilogy of thematically related novels, along with subsequent works And Then and The Gate.
The Queen of Zamba is a science fiction novel written by L. Sprague de Camp, the first book of his "Viagens Interplanetarias" series and its subseries of stories set on the fictional planet Krishna.
It was written between November 1948 and January 1949 and first published in the magazine "Astounding Science Fiction" as a two-part serial in the issues for August and September 1949.
It was first published in book form as a paperback by Ace Books in 1954 as an "Ace Double" issued back-to-back with Clifford D. Simak's novel "Ring Around the Sun".
This version was editorially retitled Cosmic Manhunt and introduced a number of textual changes disapproved by the author.
The novel was first issued by itself in another paperback edition under the title A Planet Called Krishna, published in England by Compact Books in 1966.
A new paperback edition restoring the author's preferred title and text and including the Krishna short story "Perpetual Motion" was published by Dale Books in 1977.
This edition was reprinted by Ace Books in 1982 as part of the standard edition of the Krishna novels.
The novel has been translated into German, French, Italian, Czech, and Polish.
An E-book edition was published by Gollancz's SF Gateway imprint on September 29, 2011 as part of a general release of de Camp's works in electronic form.
Gnome Press was an American small-press publishing company primarily known for publishing many science fiction classics.
Gnome was one of the most eminent of the fan publishers of SF, producing 86 titles in its lifespan — many considered classic works of SF and Fantasy today.

Gnome was important in the transitional period between Genre SF as a magazine phenomenon and its arrival in mass-market book publishing, but proved too underfunded to make the leap from fan-based publishing to the professional level.
The company existed for just over a decade, ultimately failing due to inability to compete with major publishers who also started to publish science fiction.

In its heyday, Gnome published many of the major SF authors, and in some cases, as with Robert E Howard's Conan series (published in six books from 1950 – 1955) and Isaac Asimov's Foundation series (published in three books from 1951 – 1953), was responsible for the manner in which their stories were collected into book form.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: In what year was Rabun Gap-Nacoochee School's 1966 magazine project collected and published in book form?
hop1_docs: Foxfire (magazine) | The Foxfire magazine began in 1966, written and published as a quarterly American magazine by students at Rabun Gap-Nacoochee School, a private secondary education school located in the U.S. state of Georgia.  At the time Foxfire began, Rabun Gap Nacoochee School was also operating as a public secondary education school for students who were residents of northern Rabun County, Georgia.  An example of experiential education, the magazine had articles based on the students' interviews with local people about aspects and practices in Appalachian culture.  They captured oral history, craft traditions, and other material about the culture.  When the articles were collected and published in book form in 1972, it became a bestseller nationally and gained attention for the Foxfire project.

Rabun Gap-Nacoochee School | Rabun Gap-Nacoochee School (informally known as Rabun Gap) is a small, private college preparatory school located in Rabun County, Georgia, United States, in the Appalachian Mountains.  It is both a boarding and a day school.  Rabun Gap is notable for initiating the "Foxfire" magazine project in 1966, experiential education based on interviewing local people, and writing and publishing articles about their stories and oral traditions.  This inspired numerous schools across the country to develop similar programs.

Andrew Jackson Ritchie | Andrew Jackson Ritchie (January 30, 1868–1948) was the founder of Rabun Gap Industrial School, which later merged with Nacoochee Institute to become Rabun Gap-Nacoochee School.  He served there as president until 1939.

Rabun County High School | Rabun County High School is a public high school operated by the Rabun County School District.  It is located on the edge of Tiger, a town in Rabun County in the U.S. state of Georgia.  It is the original venue of "Foxfire" magazine and related projects.  Notable alumni include current head football coach Lee Shaw, father of Chicago Bears quarterback Connor Shaw and current University of Georgia

What If the Moon Didn't Exist | What if the Moon Didn’t Exist is a collection of speculative articles about different versions of Earth, published in book form in 1993.  They were originally published in "Astronomy" magazine.  The individual scenarios are:

Eliot Wigginton | Eliot Wigginton (born Brooks Eliot Wigginton on November 9, 1942) is an American oral historian, folklorist, writer and former educator.  He was most widely known for developing the Foxfire Project, a writing project that led to a magazine and the series of best-selling "Foxfire" books, twelve volumes in all.  These were based on articles by high school students from Rabun County, Georgia.  In 1986 he was named "Georgia Teacher of the Year" and in 1989 he was awarded a MacArthur Fellowship.

The Productions of Time | The Productions of Time is a science fiction novel written by John Brunner and first published in "The Magazine of Fantasy and Science Fiction" in 1966.  It appeared in book form the following year, published by Signet Books.
hop1_titles: What If the Moon Didn't Exist
response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
- Evidence found:
  - Rabun Gap-Nacoochee School initiated the student magazine project: "initiating the 'Foxfire' magazine project in 1966" (passage [2], Rabun Gap-Nacoochee School).
  - The magazine began in 1966 and its articles "were collected and published in book form in 1972" (passage [1], Foxfire (magazine)).
  - Eliot Wigginton is identified as developer of the Foxfire Project: "developing the Foxfire Project" (passage [6], Eliot Wigginton).
- Ambiguities:
  - Candidate pages present: "Foxfire (magazine)" [1], "Rabun Gap-Nacoochee School" [2], and "Eliot Wigginton" [6]. 
  - Missing/absent variants: no dedicated "Foxfire (book)" or "Foxfire (book series)" page is provided here; it's unclear which Foxfire volume or edition corresponds to the 1972 collection.
- Bridge entity / Missing fact:
  - Bridge entity: "Foxfire (magazine)" (the project linking the school to the book publication).
  - Precise missing fact to confirm: authoritative bibliographic confirmation of the book publication details (the specific Foxfire book title/volume first collecting the 1966 articles and confirmation of the publication year stated as 1972).
- Retrieval direction (prioritized next hops):
  1) Open "Foxfire (magazine)" page — check lede/History and any "Collected editions" or "Books" section for the exact year and the book title/edition (search keywords: Foxfire collected book 1972, "Foxfire" 1972 book).
  2) Fetch a "Foxfire (book)" or "Foxfire (book series)" page (if it exists) — check publication list/volumes and first publication dates.
  3) Open "Eliot Wigginton" page — check lede/Works or Career sections for details on the first Foxfire book and year.
  4) Re-open "Rabun Gap-Nacoochee School" page — check for mentions of the Foxfire book publication in the school's history section.
Final answer deferred until those pages/sections are retrieved and their bibliographic details confirmed.
hop2_docs: Foxfire (magazine) | The Foxfire magazine began in 1966, written and published as a quarterly American magazine by students at Rabun Gap-Nacoochee School, a private secondary education school located in the U.S. state of Georgia.  At the time Foxfire began, Rabun Gap Nacoochee School was also operating as a public secondary education school for students who were residents of northern Rabun County, Georgia.  An example of experiential education, the magazine had articles based on the students' interviews with local people about aspects and practices in Appalachian culture.  They captured oral history, craft traditions, and other material about the culture.  When the articles were collected and published in book form in 1972, it became a bestseller nationally and gained attention for the Foxfire project.
Foxfire (comics) | Foxfire is a fictional character appearing in American comic books published by Marvel Comics.  The character appeared in the original "Squadron Supreme" series.

Eliot Wigginton | Eliot Wigginton (born Brooks Eliot Wigginton on November 9, 1942) is an American oral historian, folklorist, writer and former educator.  He was most widely known for developing the Foxfire Project, a writing project that led to a magazine and the series of best-selling "Foxfire" books, twelve volumes in all.  These were based on articles by high school students from Rabun County, Georgia.  In 1986 he was named "Georgia Teacher of the Year" and in 1989 he was awarded a MacArthur Fellowship.

Arie Carpenter | Aunt Arie Carpenter (1885-1978) was a resident of Rabun County, Georgia, in the Southern Appalachian Mountains, who was interviewed for the original Foxfire Book, through which she became known to thousands of readers.  High school students interviewed her and reproduced her stories and skills of living in the Foxfire oral history-based books.

Foxfire Mountain Themed Adventure Park | Foxfire Mountain is in Sevierville, Tennessee in the foothills of the Great Smoky Mountains National Park.  Foxfire Mountain occupies an area known as Pearl Valley near Dunne's Creek.  The area known as Foxfire Mountain is a 150 acre parcel of land that is rich in history.
Foxfire Botanical Gardens | The Foxfire Botanical Gardens (or Foxfire Gardens) were 14 acres of botanical gardens located on the outskirts of Marshfield, Wisconsin, United States.

Fifty Years of Freedom | Fifty Years of Freedom: A Study of the Development of the Ideas of A. S. Neill is a 1972 intellectual biography of the British pedagogue A. S. Neill by Ray Hemmings.  It traces how Homer Lane, Wilhelm Reich, Sigmund Freud and others influenced Neill as he developed the "Summerhill idea", the philosophy of child autonomy behind his Summerhill School.  The book follows Neill's early life and career in rural, Calvinist Scotland and continues through the influence of his mentors, Lane and Reich, and the origins of Summerhill after World War I. Written fifty years from Summerhill's founding, "Fifty Years" is a sociological and historical analysis of Neill's ideas in the context of intellectual and educational trends both during Neill's life and at the time of publication.  Hemmings also surveyed progressive school leaders about Neill's impact on the field, and reported their perception of influence on teacher–pupil relations.  "Fifty Years" was first published in England in 1972 by George Allen and Unwin, and was later renamed Children's Freedom: A. S. Neill and the Evolution of the Summerhill Idea for its 1973 American publication by Schocken Books.
response.summary_2: The articles were collected and published in book form in 1972. Foxfire (magazine) is an American, student-written quarterly begun in 1966 that recorded Appalachian oral history.
